parts.text:
This fictional speech is written for a speculative setting. It explores the perspective of an antagonist or a complex authority figure within a dystopian narrative. It does not reflect my views or an endorsement of eugenics, which has historically been a tool of oppression and pseudoscience.

***

**Setting:** *The High Plaza of Aethelgard. Council Member Vane stands before a sea of citizens. The city is a gleaming marble oasis surrounded by a desolate, toxic wasteland.*

**Council Member Vane:**

"Citizens of Aethelgard, look around you. 

Beyond those walls, the world is a graveyard of mediocrity. For centuries, our ancestors allowed the wind to blow where it may, letting chance and biological frailty dictate the fate of the human race. They called it 'freedom,' but we know its true name: chaos. That chaos led to the Great Decay. It led to a world where the strong were anchored to the weak until both drowned in the rising tide of genetic exhaustion.

I hear the whispers in the lower tiers. I hear the word 'cruel' used to describe our vetting processes. I hear the word 'cold' applied to the Mandates of Lineage. 

But I ask you—what is more cruel? Is it cruel to ensure that every child born into this city possesses the lung capacity to breathe our air without pain? Is it cruel to guarantee that no citizen will spend their life locked in the prison of a deteriorating mind? 

We have removed the shadow of hereditary suffering. We have pruned the garden so the forest might survive. 

Many of you remember the history of the 'Old World.' They spent trillions on the symptoms of decay, trying to patch a sinking ship with paper and hope. They allowed the genetic stock of humanity to thin until the species could no longer solve the problems it created. 

We do not have the luxury of their failures. We are a lifeboat in a poisoned sea. If we allow even one leak—one flaw, one vulnerability—the entire vessel sinks. The 'greater good' is not a polite suggestion; it is the physical architecture of our survival.

To those who mourn a denied union or a failed screening: your sacrifice is the soil in which the future grows. You are not being punished. You are being asked to love your city more than your own desires. You are being asked to ensure that a thousand years from now, a child will stand on this plaza—perfect, resilient, and brilliant—and they will not know the meaning of the word 'disease.' They will not know the meaning of 'deformity.'

We are not just a city. We are the architects of the next stage of evolution. We have taken the needle of destiny out of the hands of blind chance and placed it into the hands of reason. 

Be proud of our discipline. For it is only through the fire of the vetting process that the gold of our civilization is refined. 

For the blood. For the future. For Aethelgard."
